schools.summary: Strife traced to its inward root — not national wars but quarrels within the community ('wars,' polemoi, the settled hostility; 'fightings,' machai, the outbreaks); 'come they not hence, even of your lusts that war in your members?' — disordered cravings for pleasure and gain warring in body and soul; the pleasures first warring against the soul, the heart the battlefield before the assembly (Wesley); contrasted with the peaceable heavenly wisdom of ch3 (Ellicott).

The fruitless cycle of ungoverned desire — 'ye lust, and have not' (craving never satisfied); 'ye kill, and desire to have, and cannot obtain' (desire so violent it is murderous, in the heart by hatred, Wesley, yet failing); 'ye fight and war, yet ye have not'; wicked lust insatiable (Calvin); the diagnosis 'because ye ask not' — grasping by force replaces asking God, and a man full of evil desire cannot pray (Wesley); the failure self-inflicted.

themes: ye kill, and desire to have
ye have not because ye ask not

schools.summary: The failure of selfish prayer — 'ye ask' (some do pray) yet 'receive not, because ye ask amiss' (kakos, evilly, with a corrupt aim) 'that ye may consume it upon your lusts' (squander God's gifts on their pleasures); such would make God the minister of their own lusts (Calvin); not enough to ask, one must ask rightly, for God will not lend his bounty to feed sin.

Worldliness as spiritual adultery — 'ye adulteresses' (the better text, feminine; not literal but the prophets' figure, as Israel was espoused to God, loving the world is unfaithfulness to one's divine husband); 'the friendship of the world is enmity with God' (courting the world's favour, pleasures, and approval is war with God, the two loves cannot coexist); not coolness but active 'enmity,' the worldling an enemy of God.

debates.question: Are 'adulterers and adulteresses' literal or figurative?

sides.position: figurative — spiritual unfaithfulness to God (the prophets' image of Israel's idolatry); the true text reads 'adulteresses' only

schools.summary: One of the hardest verses — no O.T. text reads exactly thus, most taking it as a free poetic summary of Scripture's teaching (Ex 20:5; Gen 6:5) that God is jealous and man prone to envy; the debated question whose 'spirit': the human spirit (man's natural bent 'lusteth to envy,' grasping desire) or the Holy Spirit 'that dwelleth in us' who 'jealously yearns' over us for undivided love (Ellicott); both readings pressing the readers from the world to God.

sides.position: the Holy Spirit who dwells in us and jealously yearns over us for our undivided love (rebuking spiritual adultery)
the human spirit, man's natural bent, which tends toward envious, grasping desire

themes: the spirit that dwelleth in us

schools.summary: From rebuke to grace — 'he giveth more grace' (meizona, greater grace for the greater need, God's supply outstripping the demand); quoting Prov 3:34, 'God resisteth the proud' (antitassetai, sets himself in battle array against the haughty, the spirit of worldly striving) 'but giveth grace unto the humble' (the lowly who own their need); pride the root of the envious affections rebuked (Wesley); the hinge grounding the commands that follow.

The double command — 'submit yourselves to God' (hypotagete, a military word, to range oneself under God's command, the submission of the humble heart on which grace rests) and 'resist the devil' (stand firm against the tempter, instigator of the pride condemned); the promise 'he will flee from you' (Satan retreats from steadfast resistance, God never letting him conquer the man who keeps resisting, Clarke); submission first, empowering the resistance.

The gracious invitation with cleansing — 'draw nigh to God, and he will draw nigh to you' (God meets the seeking soul, like the father running to the prodigal, Luke 15:20; ready, not reluctant); priestly washings — 'cleanse your hands, ye sinners' (the outward deeds purged from evil) and 'purify your hearts, ye double minded' (the inward man purged of divided allegiance, trying to love both God and the world); a thoroughgoing repentance.

themes: he will draw nigh to you

schools.summary: The call to godly sorrow — 'be afflicted, and mourn, and weep' (not outward show but real inward grief over sin in tears; wretchedness, sorrow, tears the three steps of the homeward way, Ellicott); 'let your laughter be turned to mourning' (the carnal mirth of those who lived in pleasure exchanged for penitent sadness; the allusion to Christ's woe on those who laugh, Luke 6:25, Calvin); the godly sorrow that works repentance, not gloom for its own sake.

themes: be afflicted, and mourn, and weep

schools.summary: The commands gathered into a promise — 'humble yourselves in the sight of the Lord' (take the lowly place before God, owning one's sin in real contrition, not show before men; the root of submit/draw near/cleanse/mourn); 'and he shall lift you up' (God exalts the humble, the way down the way up; his peculiar work to raise the lowly, Calvin, fulfilling v6); echoing Christ (Mt 18:4) and Peter (1 Pet 5:6); the gospel paradox against worldly striving for eminence.

themes: in the sight of the Lord

schools.summary: The sin of evil-speaking — 'speak not evil one of another' (me katalaleite, do not slander or backbite a fellow-Christian, the wrong sharpened by 'brethren'); springing from proud hypocrisy that exalts self by disparaging others (Calvin); the argument — 'he that speaketh evil of his brother... speaketh evil of the law, and judgeth the law' (the law commands love of neighbour; the slanderer condemns and sets himself above it, not its doer but its judge), a usurpation affronting the Lawgiver.

themes: speak not evil one of another

schools.summary: The sole Lawgiver and Judge — 'there is one lawgiver' (heis, excluding all others; many texts add 'and judge'; God alone the source of law and rightful judge) 'who is able to save and to destroy' (holding every soul's eternal destiny, Mt 10:28); the withering 'who art thou that judgest another?' (what right has a creature — a poor, weak, dying worm, Wesley — to usurp God's seat?); the ground of the prohibition of slander.

themes: able to save and to destroy

schools.summary: The presumption of the self-confident planner — 'go to now' (age nyn, a call to attention); 'ye that say, To day or to morrow we will go... and buy and sell, and get gain' (mapping the future in confident detail — time, place, duration, business, profit — as if all lay in one's control); the fault not in trade itself but in planning 'as if' one had many years at one's disposal (Calvin), leaving God out of the reckoning (Barnes).

themes: go to now, ye that say
we will go... and get gain

schools.summary: The planner's frailty exposed by two facts — 'ye know not what shall be on the morrow' (the schemer cannot foresee the next day while planning a year ahead); and 'what is your life? It is even a vapour' (atmis, a mist or puff of breath, visible a moment then gone; frail, fleeting, uncertain, life utterly precarious, Clarke); how absurd to dispose of years when one cannot command the next hour; meant not to paralyse but to humble.

The cure for presumption — 'ye ought to say, If the Lord will, we shall live, and do this' (the language proper to a creature, replacing the confident boasting); a twofold acknowledgment (Calvin): 'if we live' (our continuance depends on God) and 'if the Lord will' (the success of all we undertake lies in his hand); not a mere formula but a genuine recognition of absolute dependence (Barnes); not forbidding planning but sanctifying it under God's will.

themes: we shall live, and do this

schools.summary: The evil of self-confident boasting — 'ye rejoice in your boastings' (alazoniais, braggart talk, the same word as 'the pride of life,' 1 John 2:16; taking pride in confident self-reliant schemes as if the future were secured by their own wisdom); the absolute verdict 'all such rejoicing is evil' (not merely imprudent but impious, robbing God of his government, Calvin; the boast of independence from God, Clarke); the sin in the object of the joy, glorying in self-sufficiency.

The sin of known duty left undone — 'to him that knoweth to do good, and doeth it not, to him it is sin' (the one who knows the right yet fails to do it is guilty; knowledge of duty making the omission culpable); sin not only doing the forbidden but failing the good one ought (the sin of omission); 'the ability to do good imposes an obligation to do it' (Cotton Mather, Barnes); knowledge increasing, not excusing, the condemnation (Wesley).

themes: him that knoweth to do good
